Hey, this is Russell again, and during today's interview, you guys are going to be introduced to a living legend, someone that I'm so excited to bring into this training for you guys. You will hear about this deep as we get into the interview. This is probably, of all the interviews, one of my most favorite ones, because Myron, Myron Golden is one of the most amazing people I've ever met. And we had so much fun during this interview and just goofing off and going back and forth. And I kind of initially asked him to share a couple of things, but he went deep on a whole bunch of things. I hope you guys enjoy it. The main things I wanted to talk about, number one was the constant that I learned from him called the price marinade. And I'll talk about it here in a second. And then number two was called the repitch. But he started going into like preacher closing and a whole bunch of other stuff. Like preacher closing is similar to trial closes if you watch the trial closing presentation yet, but it's the next level. Trial closing is me saying like, is this making sense? Are you guys getting this? Like getting people to say yes? What Myron does is the next level that where he tries to get other people to actually give him the presentation back. So he'll say things. Well, I won't ruin the surprise. He'll share how he does his preacher closing, but it's amazing. And it's something I haven't mastered yet. But after doing this interview, you got me re excited to go back and start implementing into my presentations as well. So the other things I'll talk about. So number one, I'm going to talk about this. We talk about some of these bonus videos is your presentation timeline. Right? So here's the timeline of your presentation. You've got your one thing, your three secrets, your stack and your clothes and those things like that. Right? So the very beginning of the presentation is where you can plug in a price marinade. Sorry, P and this is the price marinade that Myron will be talking about. The first time that I did the price marinade correctly was at the 10X event. And you hear me talk about it in the initial trainings. The price marinade is all about like, figure out where you're going to sell and giving it the value of what it's worth and like having people get the sticker shock up front like, oh my gosh, like I can't afford that. Right? And they kind of put it on the shelf, but then you start teaching and start and start and you're going to give them a doctorate and tell them the stories and break their faults, believe patterns and all these things are happening in the timeline and the whole time what's there is a listing to you as you're going through this timeline. They're trying to figure out in their mind like, how could I pay for that? How could I pay for that? I'm trying to figure out, you know, if I move my money from here to here and like in their mind, they're figuring out how they can do it and then you come back with the actual price at the end, which is less, and then they've been like, let's say you've narrated the price at $10,000, the whole time in their head, there's 10 grand, 10 grand, all of a sudden like, oh it's only three. It's like, oh I could totally do three. Right? works because you set up front, you give them the sticker shock, you rip that off immediately at the very beginning, then you come back later and then you drop the price down. So price married, he'll talk about the strategy, how it works, how you developed it, those kind of things, number one. Then the preacher closing will be happening all throughout. You guys can weave that in with your trial closes. You learn from the Ted Thomas interview. And the last thing we talked about is the concept of a repitch. So repitch, luckily for me at Grant Cardone's event, they allowed me to repitch. So I spoke the very first time, did my presentation, we sold a bunch of sales, and then the next day I had a chance to come back and do, I think he gave me like six minutes to do a repitch, which is me kind of trying to do one more push to get people through. And if you look at like the timeline of how we sell things, right, things start out initially, we try to sell through emotion, right? So I'll put a big E here. Emotion is usually the first close. So if you look at our core presentations, the emotional argument, I'm trying to emotionally sell somebody. And then the majority of our buyers come from the emotional close. But then there's a lot of people there who emotionally felt it, but they have to logically convince themselves, right? And so that's the next wave of people who come by, the people who are trying to logically buy something. And so what happens is for the repitch I come back, and I usually make an appeal to people's logic, like logically it makes sense that if you want to do this in your business, like I'm the person to learn from, logically it makes sense that if you're going to try to text your company next 10 months or 12 months, logically you need something bigger than just like changing your sales script. You need more leads, more traffic, more things than so funnels of things. So I'm talking logically to the logical person who the night before emotionally wanted to buy it, but they logically talked themselves out of it. I'm trying to logically talk them back into it, right? And then the third phase then in selling is US, urgency and scarcity. So when I'm selling anything, this is for any kind of presentation, starting emotions, that's my best shot at selling someone, moving to logic and then ending with urgency and scarcity, right? So in an event, a physical event, I go and I do my presentation, which all these things will be weaved into the presentation, but for the most part, this is an emotional sell. And then the next sell, so the repitch, I came back the next day and I made a logical and urgency and scarcity, right? If you look at the repitch, it was like logically you need this stuff and you got to buy it right now because I'm leaving in two hours and I don't know if your reform is gone, right? So I'm appealing to logic and urgency and scarcity, which for us drove another $250,000 in sales at the 10X event. At our Funnel Hacking Live event, I did my initial presentation and my owner will share the numbers with you during the interview, but we did around $4 million in sales. He came back the next day and did a logical repitch and during the logical repitch and we brought in urgency and scarcity, he did a $3 million in sales doing the repitch, okay? So that's called the repitch over here on TCH. In fact, what we're going to be doing, which is kind of exciting, he allowed me to do this is I'm going to take his 30-minute presentation for Funnel Hacking Live, I'm actually going to plug it into the interview. So you'll have a chance to see him actually do it and you'll see how did he do $3.5 million in 30 minutes? Like you'll see how he did it. And notice he's doing that like, how do you appeal to, I mean, he talked emotionally as well, but he's also appealing to logic and urgency and scarcity in this presentation, right? So that, I'm plugging that part from Funnel Hacking Live into this interview, which is insanely cool. But then I always want you to think, and some of you guys again are not going to be using this to sell on stage, you're going to be doing webinars. So the webinar sequence, right? Hey, everyone, this is Russell Brunson and welcome you to the training for today. I am here with one of the most amazing closers on webinars and stage presenting that I've ever seen in my life. He's someone who recently after I thought I took every dollar out of a room, he stood up on stage for 30 minutes the next day and pulled another three and a half million dollars out of the same people for the same program. He is someone who I look up to and respect more than almost anybody else on this planet. His name is Myron Golden. Myron, how are you doing, man? I'm utterly fantastic and better now that I'm talking to RB, the triple profits maker. Oh, yeah. That's being a lot of way. That'd be the new name. Yeah. Just made that up. RB, the triple profit maker. I'm going to buy the domain. I like that. So to jump off, for those who don't know you, do you want to kind of give a real quick recap of your journey into this world and how you got to be R&M? Yeah, I got started in network marketing back in 1985. My first job that I worked out after I got married was driving a trash truck for $6.25 an hour. I did that during the day, came home, took a nap, then went out and sold financial services at night. And to encourage people, like there are a lot of people who are watching this, I'm sure, who are not that great at sales right now. But I promise you, if you will be willing to stay bad long enough to get good, like keep state in the process, even though you're not good at it, you can eventually become great at sales. I was horrible. When I first got started selling financial services, started, this might be free to believe it literally took me 18 months to make my first sale. And I was working. It wasn't like 18 months of just sitting back, waiting to get paid. No, I was talking to people almost every day and just, I didn't, I didn't understand what was going on in the whole sales process. But guess what happens when you do that many presentations over that long on time period? You take the feedback and then you go back and you do it again and you change your approach and you do it again. And I kept doing that until I made a sale. Then I made another one. I made a bunch more. I became the number one salesperson in our office and I eventually got to the place where selling became as easy as falling down the steps. It was fascinating. All the people I've been interviewing for this, um, all people I respect at this level now, all of them have similar story where like it was hard at first. They kept doing it and doing it. And it was repetition that they got people's success. Like Jamie Cross out of this story, she told me the other day was cool. She was like out, um, at farmer markets every single day, all summer long, selling her soap and she was like doing them and demos and like every time someone says them, she remembered that and write notes of them, like changing the pitch and changing the pitch and changing it. Like for three years doing that farmers markets, then she stepped up and did a webinar and it worked. And you're like, oh, you're overnight success source. Like, no, I was like at a farmer market every single day learning this. You went through the same process through financial services and like, um, it's fascinating how I think we all think it's just going to be like, oh, but like to, to really master it does take the repetition of the time to kind of, absolutely. But you want to talk, so the price of marinade is like, you told me that and then I didn't dare do it for like, I don't know, four or five years because it scared me and intimidated me. I was like that, I don't have the guts for that. And then the very first time I did it, as you know, was at the 10 X event. And I was so scared. I literally, I built out the slides. I had him in there. I was practicing in my hotel room and I was about to feel that moment. We have to like download your slides to USB drive to get to the dude who's going to put it on the screen. And I was like about to copy it over. I was like, I'm going to leave these slides. I don't like, I don't dare to do it for all these people. And I was going to highlight him. I was like, and then I was like, I'm just going to do it. Like whatever. And I didn't copy slides over, gave the guy, he ran out the door to go put it in. And I was like, I'm going to do the price marinated for all these people. And it's really bad or really good. But when you talk about, I don't know, like maybe when you came up with it, but like I love the history behind it. You came up with it and then how you use it so people can start using it in their presentations. Yeah. So I just realized that when people are listening to us do a presentation, they're thinking about something, right? And they're usually analyzing during every part of the process of our presentation, whether or not they're going to buy at the end. Right. And so I thought, wow, what if I build up all of this? What if I give them the price, a price upfront that is going to be ultimately higher than the final price I'm going to charge them, right? Cause everybody loves the deal. So, so even before I get there, one of the things I'm going to say about selling anything is if you want to make a lot of money in sales, you got to learn to work with human nature instead of working against it. And a lot of people have this vested interest in being right, right? So they want to be right. Well, I'm not going to do that because that's not blah, blah, blah. And they've got all this conversation going on in their head. But the reality is human nature says people love a deal. Well, people love a deal and that helps them buy. Why would I not create a deal so it makes it easier for them to say yes. So everything that I'm, I'm doing in my presentation is to lead to the easy yes. I want them to, I want it to make it easy for them to say yes. Right. So, so that's, that's the thought in my mind. That's where I'm, that's where I'm at. So people love a deal. So that's what the price marinade is based on. So I look at the fact they're going to be thinking about something while I'm presenting. If I can uncover enough value in this presentation, cause, cause most people misunderstand sales, I was just speaking to like 500 people down here in this hotel in London, right? And so, um, I was telling them, most people think that selling is talking to people into buying things they don't want, don't need or can't afford. That's not selling. Selling is uncovering the value of what we have to offer so well that people are happy to exchange the money they have in their pocket to the, for the value we've revealed. So get good at uncovering the value. So I said, if I can like give them a price that's bigger than the final price is going to be at the offer, and then I can uncover enough value during my presentation that at least matches this price, this big price that I give them at the beginning. Then when I finally give them the deal price at the end, if it's longer than, if it's lower than the marinade price, the price, and the reason I call it a marinade is because I give them this big price and I let it soak in. I let that big price soak in. I want them thinking the whole time I'm doing a presentation. I've got to, where can I get this money? I got, like, I want to give them so much value in presentation thinking, okay, like move some money from over here and get that. And then by the time I give them a price, it's lower than that. Like, Oh, I got that much in my wallet. Let me go buy this thing. So that's the whole mentality behind the price marinade. It's marinating. They're marinating where they're going to get the money from because you're going to, you're going to stack impact so much value into the presentation and what you're teaching them. But by the time you get done, they already know they want to get it. They just need to figure out where to get the money. Yeah. Does that make sense? So good. Um, the first time I tested, I did it wrong. You told me later I did it wrong, but I got close. This was a movie, our old certification thing. And I priced marinated for the actual price it was at the end. So I totally messed it up. But I was like, I told them that the program's $10,000 to get started. Then the rest of my presentation was to show them a whole bunch of different ways that they could make $10,000 to one client. That's there's like, if I see one client is free and that then also 10,000, which seemed like a huge number at the beginning by the end was like, Oh, it's nothing peanuts. Yeah. So that's, that's a, so exciting. Um, very, very cool. Now, um, you use it not only just like in a, like in a 90 minute presentation, though, cause you do events that are multiple days. How do you use it in an event like that? Where it's not just like getting an end, but it's, it's, it's, you know, the whole storylines weaving for more than a day. Yeah. So, so the price marinade can manifest itself in a couple of different ways. Um, you can price marinate the, can price marinate the retail price over the sales price, right? You can price marinate the bonuses and then the bonuses that you're going to give them, like really uncover the value of those bonuses and what they're going to get out of those bonuses. And then when you do the offer and the offer is less than the price of the bonuses and you throw the bonuses in for free, that's, that creates an explosion of sales. It's just goes insane when you price marinate the bonuses. So you actually tell them the price of the bonuses, the retail price of the bonuses, and then when you do your presentation and they're thinking about having to buy all these different things. And by the time you get down, you do your stack and the walk down when you go, it's this, this, this, this, this, this, but I'm going to give you a discount because of this. And then you get down to the bottom and you say, okay, everybody gets this. I'm going to throw in these bonuses that are the thing they already want anyway. That's another way to do the price. Another way to do the price marinade is the selection close. I like to save that close, um, that price marinade for a multi-day event where I'm selling something that's 25, 35, 50, a hundred thousand dollars. Right. I save that price marinade. I call it the selection close because one of the things I discovered, if, if, if you think of, I don't think of selling as hard, but a lot of people do. So if you, if you think, so, um, one of the best ways for you not to have, have to ask them to pay you is you get them to ask you if they can buy. So what do I mean? So at the beginning of an event where I know I'm going to sell a high price program on the first day, I'll say, you know, I've got a program that, you know, it's not, it's not cheap. It's less, a little bit less than a house, well, a little bit less than some houses. Or, you know, it's less than a car. What depends on the car is definitely less than an S550. It's less than Lamborghini. So I'll, I'll, I'll paint a picture and I'll say, I've got this training that I'm going to do, like if I were price marinating, um, my speaker training, for instance, I would say I've got the speaker training, um, that is regularly, um, we'll just say $53,000, right? And I'll teach you how to sell from stage. So you say, well, that's a lot of money. Well, then I would tell them stories about clients of mine who made way more than that, the first event, right? So I'm price marinating that big price. And then what I do is I tell them, I say, here's what you do. Um, I want you to write me an essay. If you want to be selected by me and you want me to help you make, you want me to help you make $300,000 in a weekend, like I just helped one of my students do. If you want me to help you make $100,000 a weekend, like I just hope one of my students do, you want me to help you make like, like $5 million in a weekend, like I hope one of my students do, and I want you to write me an essay. It can be a paragraph. It can be a page. I don't want to be more than a page front and back. That's the most. And I want you to tell me why I should select you because I'm not working with everybody in this room. And so I know a premium product like that, usually about 10% of the room is going to be max. It'll usually be more like 8% of the room if I'm selling like a $50,000 program. So I'll tell them, I say, I want you to convince me that I should let you take this program because I don't want to sell it to somebody who's not going to do it. I don't need your money and you don't need to spend the money if you're not going to do the work. So if you think that you deserve to work with me and have one of the best stage presenters, stage closers in the world, teach you how to make hundreds of thousands or millions of dollars an hour, then write me an essay. Make sure it's turned into the day. If you turn it into tomorrow morning, you're already disqualified yourself. Right. And so, and so I have people come and give me that. So that's how I price marinate that. And I watch what I do the next day. I read all of them that night. And the next day I'll call people up in front of the room. And get them to sell me in front of everybody else on why I should select them. I mean, the consistency, right? And so people come up and say, well, the reason you should say like me is because I'm going to be on a scale of one to 10, how serious are you? Oh, I'm so serious. I'm willing to do whatever it takes. Like, see, some people are not coachable. And I'll say, I'll say this. Some people are not coachable. They pretend to be coachable. They think they're coachable. They're really not coachable. On a scale of one to 10, how coachable are you? Let me tell, let me help you understand what that means. And so, uh, we got back on stage and I'll tell kind of the, the aftermath and we'll go into like what actually happened, but you got on stage for basically 30 minutes, um, told some stories. I like, I had one of my best friends who's been with me for like 10 years who didn't buy for me. And then he bought one of Myron's folks. Myron then did this at Dana Derek's event. And I remember Dana came up to me after Myron did the repitch and came up to, to, um, to me and was like, dude, my mom bought for Myron. My mom didn't buy for me, but she bought. And, um, and so there's something there is happening. And I, and I kind of understand, but not super well. Um, we're going back into all of our webinars now, we're doing the webinar live and then there's like, they're structurally in my brain. There's a repitch that's happening in a different sequence later because it's like the power of it's so strong. I love you to talk about just some of like kind of the concept and what's happening where I'm selling day one, the repitch happening day two. And then like when you were doing that and kind of doing the story, like, how do you think through it? What are you trying to do during the, during the repitch? Um, so the people who are there, who didn't buy, here's the, the, here's the, here's the, the presupposition that I'm operating from. The people who are there, who haven't bought wanted to buy. So when I think of selling, I think of, I think of a force selling, especially to a group is a four stage process, but even individually. So the first thing I want to do is I want to get them to enroll, right? Enroll in what I want them to get to enroll, which means agree with learning the way I teach and I teach with a lot of interaction. So that's the first thing I want to enroll them in, in interaction. So the second thing I want to do is I want to get them to engage. And that is keeping them responding. The best way in the world to make sure the best way to give a presentation is to get your prospect, whether one on one or two on live audience, get your prospect to give your presentation to themselves. Because if I say it, they can disagree with me, but if they say it, they can't disagree with themselves without being schizophrenic. If I say it, right, they can hear me or might not hear me, but if they say it, they have to hear them. So I get them to say what I want them to hear. And you do that makes. Explain, I've seen you do it. Explain how you do that. Okay. So, so what I do is I'll say, um, I am not a lecturer. I'm not a what? I'm not a lecture. I'm not a lecturer, which means I don't give lectures. A lecture is somebody standing in front of the room and they're saying something they're assuming that because they're saying you're here, I am not a lecturer. I use a concept called natural learning. What's it called? It's called. Natural learning. Natural learning. The institutional learning is one person talking a whole bunch of people allegedly learning, but natural learning is play. It's having fun. Little puppies don't learn to be big dogs by going to big dog school and listening to a big dog, give a monologue. Okay. Little puppies, I mean, little kittens don't go to, um, big cat school and learn how to be cats. They learn how to be cats by pouncing on each when their kittens pouncing on each other, they learn how to be big dogs by when they're little puppies, they chase each other. And what you've got to learn to do is you've got to learn how to play. So we're going to play today. We're going to have a lot of fun. How many of you like fun learning better than you like boring learning? Say me, me. Exactly. So I get them. So now what that means is as we're doing natural learnings, I'm natural learning. I'm going to ask a lot of questions. Can I ask a lot of what? Questions. Questions. Number two, if I make a statement, I'm going to get you to repeat it. I'm going to get you to do what? Repeat it. I'm going to get you to repeat it. The reason I'm getting you to repeat it is so you hear it. I already know this stuff. How many already learned this stuff? Say yes. Yes. That's why I'm going to get you to repeat it. And then third thing, if I begin a sentence, leave the last word off, I'm going to get you to fill in the blank. Blank. Very good. Give yourselves a hand. You've got the process. All right. So there they clap and now they're enrolled. They're enrolled and I just keep doing that over and over again. And if I tell them something that makes sense, you got, if you, if you really want to make more money and you want to take your life to another level, you've got to ask better questions so you can get better answers. Ask better what? Question. Questions so you can get better. What? Answer. Excellent. And you deserve better answer. How many of you know that? Say I do, I do. And I literally get them to do, I call that choir director closing. Right. I make them say back to me. What I'm saying to them. I call that the choir director. Now, but so, so think about how powerful this concept is. If you think about the laws of physics, right? You've got most, you've got momentum, the law of momentum. You've got the law of inertia. Think about this is in selling because all principles are microcosms of each other. So think about this momentum. The law of momentum says an object in motion will continue in motion. It will pick up speed and velocity until and unless it's acted upon by an outside force. So if I can get something going, it's easier for me to keep it going than it is to get it stopped. Right. Mm hmm. But the law of inertia says an object at rest will remain at rest until and unless it's acted upon by an outside force. So think about this. If I'm doing a presentation and people are watching me do a presentation, I have momentum, they have inertia. Mm hmm. So they're listening to me talk for 90 minutes and they're doing nothing. And now all of a sudden in the last five minutes or 15 minutes of my talk, I want to overcome the inertia that I created and now I want them to have momentum. Like I'm working against the laws of nature. That's like throwing bricks over my head and hoping they don't come down and crush my skull. Right. So, so what I'm going to do, I'm going to create momentum in the beginning. If I get them responding throughout my presentation, responding in the clothes is just the natural continuation of that. So that's what engaging means. So I enroll, engage. Next thing I got to do is I got to educate. Got to teach them something. So you want their mind going off like popcorn. So the third thing, so the first thing is enroll, second thing is engaged. Third thing is educate. You got to teach really cool stuff. And by the way, one of the coolest ways you can teach them really cool stuff is you've got to like, don't go around repeating what you think you heard somebody else say, because most of the time it wasn't that good when the first person said, right? So when you do your version, it's going to get worse and worse and worse. So what you do, the way I see, I like pay attention, like pay attention to the things that show up in your life and see how you navigate through them and take notes on that. Do you remember Charlie's Tremendous Jones? You remember him or not really? Is that before your time? Okay. So Charlie, Charlie Tremendous Jones was a motivational speaker back during the days of like Earl Nightingale and all those guys, right? But his thing was he loved books. He loved introducing people to books and he used to have a bookstore in our town in my Canterbury, Pennsylvania, but he shipped hundreds of thousands, millions of books all around the world. Charlie wrote a book called Life is Tremendous. Have you ever read that? No. No, you know. Okay. So like I read that when I was like 21, right? And the thing that I remember from that book to this day, like it was a lot of things, Charlie was just a great guy. He exemplified enthusiasm. And that's why his nickname was Charlie Tremendous Jones. So Charlie wrote this book called Life is Tremendous. And in that book, one of the things he said, he had this really, really cool old guy voice and he said, he said, when you, when you read a book or when you go to church and you hear a sermon or you hear a seminar, when somebody says something that you really like, don't write down what they said, write down what you thought of as a result. But what they said, and I'm telling you that sentence right there has made me millions of dollars because of writing down what I thought of as a result of people, what I heard people say. So remember I showed you this book, you square. Yep. Mary showed you you square, right? And I showed you my notes, right? Yep. And you got yours. Oh, mine's here. That too. So I showed you my notes. So when I write, when I read something, see those yellow sticky notes? Sometimes they're green. Sometimes you're yellow, but you see those yellow sticky notes? Yeah. Those are my Charlie Jones thoughts. I call it, I call it coming, I call it Charlie Jones in it. So what I do is when I read a read something that somebody said, and I like the way they said it, I think, okay, why did I like that so much? Okay. I like that because, and then I figure it out. And then that's the thing I write down. I don't ever write down somebody's quote, mom taking notes. I write down what I think of. That's how I create so much original content. Like, you know that, and I'm not patting myself on the back, but when I talk, like I say stuff, I don't know where it comes from, but it comes out, right? Some stuff comes out. And so that's a result of 40 years, not 40 years, how old am I? 57, almost 40 years, like, like 30 something years of Charlie Jonesing, listening stuff, putting it in my own words, putting down what it means to me. And then I say stuff, people are like, oh, that's the greatest thing that ever I've ever heard. Thank you for melting my mind. And so, so that's educating people, right? You want to educate people and it's good if you can do it in epigrams. Like there's no strength without struggle. If you can tack a couple of them together, it's like boom, boom, boom. There's no strength without struggle. There's no destiny without difficulty. There's no advancement without adversity. You got to go through some stuff to get to your stuff. And when you say, oh my goodness, that's amazing. Now people already know that, but they feel it when you can put it, when you can make it succinct and powerful, they can feel it, right? So, so educate. And then the last piece of closing, especially from the stage, is you empower people. What do you mean? Empower them. You empower them to take the action they desire to take. What is that? You remove all of the reasons why they can't. That's what my storytelling is all about. It's all about most of the storytelling is about the empowering things. I'm empowering them to take the action. I know they desire to take. How do I know they desire to take? Because even if they didn't have it when they came in, first of all, if like people were in Dana Derek's room, when we were at Dream 100, they were there because they wanted to do that, learn that how to dream 100. So I didn't know that they wanted Dana's offer. I operate from the standpoint that people already desired the thing or they wouldn't even be in the room. Right. And now I've got 90 minutes to build even more desire on top of their initial desire. Now they want it more. So if they're not buying, why are they not buying? They're not buying because they've got some disempowering questions in their mind. For instance, disempowering question, no matter what. What if this doesn't work? So what do I do? I disempower the disempowering question. I take all of the power out of the disempowering question. That's the whole purpose of the story. Right. So, and I'll tell them, um, what if it doesn't work? Well, you already know what happens if it doesn't work. You already have that. And the question is, do you want to keep living with it? So learn to ask yourself better questions instead of asking yourself, what if this doesn't work? Now, see most people think the opposite of what, what if it doesn't work? Most people think the opposite of that as well. What if it does? That's not a good question. It's not a disempowering question, but it's not empowering. Here's an empowering question. How great is my life going to be when this works? You see what I'm saying? So I'm not just, I'm not just taking the disempowering question and flipping it over. I'm, I'm taking the disempowering question and I'm asking a question that actually makes an empowering statement. And that's why I do the two biggest expenses close. I take all the power out of the, what if it doesn't work? Does that make sense? Yeah. What are you too big? What are the two most expensive things you currently pay for? How many of you have a hard time with the concept of selling? Come on, keep it real with me, my people. Okay. And I told you guys, when I first started selling, it took me a year and a half to make my first sale, but one of the reasons it was hard for me is because I wasn't really convinced that selling was something good to do for people. See, in life, you don't get what you want. You get what you are. God, here's how God set it up. Everything reproduces after its own tree. You will never, after its own time. You will never see an orange growing on an apple tree. You'll never see an apple growing on an orange tree. How many of you would like to have 3000 people who paid you $1,000 to come to your event? Yes? Okay. How many of you would like to have, have 100 people by your $18,000 program? Right? You don't get what you want. You get what you are. There are four categories of people out there in the marketplace. You've got freeple people. You know who freeple people are, don't you? They're the people who want everything for free. But if you are freeple people, you will never be able to sell anybody anything because it's against your, you'll have too much internal conflict. The next level is cheapo people. Right? They want anything as long as it's cheap. Right? They go through life telling themselves the lie that they can't afford it. So since they believe the lie that they can't afford it, they also believe that other people can't afford it. So, so let me, let me just like totally destroy the myth of I can't afford it. Everybody ready? Say I'm ready. Okay. People don't buy what they can afford. People buy the things they want the most. Do you want to change your family's life? You want to take epic world class vacations? Do you want to impact the world in a great way? Then what you've got to do is stop being freeple and stop being cheapo and level up so you can learn how to use your mouth and your mind to create the wealth that your family deserves and you desire. Does that make sense? Everyone say yes. Yeah. So if you, and this is not, this is not me saying, I'm not, I have no convincing blood in me. Like I don't want to convince anybody anything. Like if you're not, if you're not sharp enough to see that $10 million is more than you're making now, then maybe, maybe I just can't help you. Right? But there's freeple people. There's cheapo people. And see, here's, here's the lie that cheapo people believe. Or here's, here's the reality about cheapo people that they don't realize that the only thing that perpetuates poverty is the expectation of poverty. What do I mean by that? See, cheapo people and freeple people ask the wrong question. If you change the question you ask, if you haven't already, if you already haven't signed up to go on the cruise with us and be at the luncheon with us and all the rest of that, if you haven't already done that because you're asking yourself the wrong question. Here's the question you're asking yourself. What if it doesn't work? No, seriously, I want you to think about that. You're saying, well, what, but what if I pay Russell $1,800 a month and it doesn't work? What if I pay him $18,000 and it doesn't work? No, no, that's not the question. See, you already know what you're already doing. Already isn't working. So the real question that you should ask is what? What if this works? What if it works? How much better will I be able to take care of my family when this works? How much better is life going to be when this works? I'm going to tell you something. Hey, folks, I'm going to keep it real. I grew up poor. I grew up poor. I mean, like my parents never bought a brand new car the whole time. Any of me and my six brothers were living in their house our whole lives because six, seven boys can eat a lot of food. Right. They never bought a brand new car. I didn't even know people bought brand new cars. I remember when my brothers got jobs selling cars, I thought they were going to go broke. Nobody buys new cars, right? And what I want you to understand is I had polio as an infant. There were times in my life where I couldn't get a job. I don't want to. I don't know if I should tell you all that or not, but we were poor after I first made this beautiful girl, we were broke. It was bad. I want to tell you something. There was nothing I wouldn't do to change my family's life. I've taken the first time I got a chance to take my family to Hawaii. We stayed there for a whole month and we made up our mind. We weren't coming back until we were sick of Hawaii. How much is that worth? So, so don't ask the question, what if it doesn't work? Ask the question, what if it does? How is it? How much better is my life going to be when this works? This is going to be so great. It's really interesting. And it's really interesting that the expectation of poverty or focus on poverty, how it creates poverty. I'll say this and then I'll let Russell and you guys decide whether you want me to tell you the rest of it, but I'm going to tell you something that's amazing. The very first temptation in the history of the world, in the Garden of Eden with Adam and Eve, very first temptation. Are you ready for this? It was the temptation to focus on lack. It was what? I know y'all are going to say, come on, Myron. That's a little much, isn't it? Right? Do y'all want me to share that with you real quick or not really? Okay. Is that okay? Yeah. Just real quick. So God told Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden, he said, of every tree of the garden, every tree of the garden, you may freely eat. Now let me ask you a question about those two words, every and freely, or those abundance words or lack words? Those are abundance words. And then he said, of the tree, the tree of knowledge of good and evil, don't eat off that tree. That, how many trees is that? One. So they had everything freely and they only lacked one thing. And here's where the, here's where the tree of knowledge of good and evil was. It was in the middle of the garden. Right? And all these other trees were all around here, all around here. And so the devil comes and says, he comes and says, Hey, Eve, didn't God say you can't eat off of every tree of the garden? You know what he did? He took away one word and then added the word not. And she said, Hmm. And then she said, we may either the fruit of the trees of the garden. She took away two words and it says, and when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, one desires to make one wise, she took the fruit there of and did eat, gave all sort of to her husband with her and he did it. And once it gets down in the ground, here's what it's got to do. It's got to die, but it's got to do more than that. Y'all ready? It has to be willing to cease to be a seed. And in order for you to become everything you can become, you must be willing to stop being what you have been so you can become all that you can be. And it goes down into the ground and it dies and it stops being a seed. It's no longer a seed. And then it develops a root system. It grows up. It becomes a tree. I'm running away tonight. I'm not doing this anymore. And all of a sudden, the wind catches the wings just right. That baby starts floating. He's like, I believe I can fly. I believe I can touch the sky. The eagle learns to fly by falling. And even an eagle, before it goes up, it has to go down. But you know what? Here's what human beings do. They live in a nest. That nest is called the circle of sameness. And when it's our turn to learn to fly, we start falling. Do all human beings learn to fly as high as they possibly can? Nope. You know why? Because we have a choice. Eagles don't have a choice. What percentage of eagles learn how to fly? 100%. But what percentage of people learn to max out their potential? Not nearly enough. So the anticipation of impact is the thing we're afraid of. People say, I'm afraid to fall. You're not afraid of falling. You're afraid that you're going to hit the ground. And so while we're falling, instead of allowing the wind to catch our wings, we grab onto the lowest hanging branch. We crawl back up into the circle of sameness called the comfort zone. And we live the rest of our lives there. If before they build a building, they've got to dig a hole in the ground and put a foundation down. If a seed has to go on the ground and stop being a seed, if an airplane has flaps that have to go down, anything that goes up has to go down. So you didn't even know you just got double tax on the same money. They tax you if you sell the thing for more than you bought it for, that's called capital gains tax. I wanna talk about, because then a lot of people watch this, may not ever do a stage presentation. They may never have a chance for a repitch. Like at Grant Cardone's event, they allowed me to come back and do a repitch, which was cool. But most places don't let you do that. But in the context of, let's say we're doing webinars or auto webinars, I know how we're using now, but how would you use something like this, like a repitch in an actual webinar sequence? You know, that's a really great question. I think one of the things you could do is you could just do like the day after, even the day after a webinar, do like a Q&A. Do a Q&A and every time somebody asks you a question, it gives you an opportunity to do, to repitch, to answer that question. Because here's one of the things I noticed about people that have questions. In fact, you might take all, oh, that's sort of a great idea. You take all of the questions that people ask during the webinar. And the next day, like your answer's as many as you can, is the next day you say, look, here's what I'm gonna do. I'm gonna take some time tomorrow at such and such a time, I'm gonna answer all these questions. Or you don't even say it on the webinar, you just send them an email and say, hey, I had the great questions that people ask, blah, blah, blah, you build it up. I'm gonna answer these questions tonight that are gonna help you get to whatever your goal is at the webinar, right? And then you go back on there, because here's what I discovered. If there's one person asking your question, there's at least 10 people that have it. So if you answer it for that one, you're answering it for 10 more people also. So that's one of the things that I would recommend for doing a repitch. And I mean, sending out a rep, even just sending out the replay is good. But I think if you just go live again, and just maybe go live in a different platform, go live on a Facebook live. You did your webinar on a webinar, go live on a Facebook live to answer the question. You say, hey, I did this webinar last night, and people ask me all these great questions that I wanna answer. And man, I think that will help you a lot if you're there and send that out to the people who showed up for the webinar, the people who didn't show up for the webinar, and then get on and answer those questions by telling stories that disempower, they're disempowering beliefs. I think it'd be ginormous once again. Yeah, it's funny, somebody told me today, they'd done a webinar, and they did a bunch of them, they said, Russell, in your book, you say that usually you double the sales of a webinar after the webinar is over from replays and something like that. And they, I never believed, I never did it. And they finally this time, I did an actual sequence, and they literally did more sales from the replay sequence than before. Absolutely. And a big piece of it is because of that, the people that buy the first time, a lot of times it's people that come in and they're emotionally charged, they're hot aspires, and they get excited and they'll go and buy. And there's other people that get off the webinar and they sit at home and they're sitting in bed and they're thinking about it, and they're talking to their spouse about it, and they're contemplating, and they're like the more logical thinkers that are trying to figure things out. And coming back with that second thing gives you the ability now to respond to those questions differently and be able to touch people in a different way that they may not have said yes for the first time just because of whatever it is. I mean, all of us function differently, we process things in our minds differently. I talked with a little bit about the event last weekend, like my first patch is like, how do I sell people emotionally? So most people buy emotionally. And after we've done that and we got those people out of remits, like now let's come back, logically explain to the next set of people, and then the last set is like urgency and scarcity. So I look at my sequences, it's like for me, the webinar or the stage pitch is the emotional sell. Then the next part is the logical sell, and then the last part is like, the urgency and scarcity is taken away from them, that's the best way to get someone to off the fences.
